coredata.dc:title: Gradient heating-induced bi-phase synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) on graphene-coated carbon cloth for efficient photoelectrocatalysis
coredata.dc:creator: Ali, Mumtaz
Anjum, Aima Sameen
Bibi, Ayesha
Wageh, S.
Sun, Kyung Chul
Jeong, Sung Hoon
coredata.dc:description: The challenges of secondary pollution and limited stability of metallic catalysts/quantum dots that are used for water treatment must be resolved in the emerging ecofriendly environmental systems. Conversely, carbon materials, specifically, conventional carbon quantum dots (C-CQDs) have emerged as an abundant, stable, and biocompatible alternative for visible-light-driven photocatalysts, that are used for water treatment. Despite these advantages, the fast charge recombination in quantum-confined systems, complex purification, and limited optoelectronic performance are bottlenecks in the practical application of C-CQDs. To address these issues, we proposed a scalable structural design of C-CQDs with enhanced photocatalytic properties. The synthesis process of CQDs was modified to yield a highly amorphous core carbon quantum dots (AC-CQDs), which was controlled by varying the synthesis temperature. The low initial temperature during the synthesis of the AC-CQDs yields an amorphous core, which provides a high electrical resistance; hence, the indirect recombination occurring through core conductivity is significantly suppressed. To ensure scalable synthesis and stability, AC-CQDs were directly grown on reduced graphene oxide, which was coated on a carbon fabric to fabricate a textile-structured electrode. Efficient charge separation in the proposed catalyst electrode structure offers significantly improved photoelectrocatalytic activity, i.e., 100% effluent dye degradation in 25 min.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of conventional carbon quantum dots (C-CQDs) on rGO
Synthesis of amorphous-core carbon quantum dots (AC-CQDs) on rGO
Mechanism of catalysis and CQDs structure

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Temperature-dependent crystal structure formation in conventional carbon quantum dots (C-CQDs). Florescent centers of each type of CQDs is given along with (top). (b) Structural comparison of C-CQDs with amorphous-core carbon quantum dots (AC-CQDs).

(a) Schematic illustration of the experimental design. Graphene oxide was synthesized from graphite, which served as a substrate for the synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs). Different morphologies of CQDs were formed by different carbonization temperatures. (b) Major steps involved in the formation of CQD-modified reduced graphene oxide (rGO) electrodes.

Schematic illustration and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis of quantum dots grown on rGO, (a) C-CQDs, which are synthesized at a constant temperature of 180 °C. (b) AC-CQDs, which are synthesized at an initial low temperature and final high temperature. (c) TEM of bare rGO sheets. (d–f) TEM images of the C-CQDs grown on rGO (CD@rGO) measured from lower to higher resolutions. (g–i) TEM images of the AC-CQDs grown on rGO (AD@rGO) measured from lower to higher resolutions. TEM scale bar is 5 nm for high-resolution images (a, b, h, and i).

Characterizations of quantum dot-modified rGO. Scanning electron microscopy analysis of (a) carbon cloth (CC), (b) bare rGO on CC, (c) CD@rGO coated on CC, and (d) AD@rGO coated on CC. (e) Wide-angle X-ray diffraction survey of the composite structures. (f) Comparison of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analyses and (g) deconvoluted O 1s peaks of the composite structures.

(a) Ultraviolet (UV)-visible absorbance of the colloidal solution of CQDs. (b) Time-resolved photoluminescence spectra of the CQD colloid. (c, d) Photoluminescence emission spectra for C-CQDs and AC-CQDs.

(a) Photocatalytic activity of the composite electrodes. (b) UV–visible light absorption of the dye after 10 min of degradation. (c) Cyclic voltammograms of the composite electrodes. (d) Nyquist plots of the composite electrodes.

(a) Mott–Schottky plot of the electrodes. (b) UV–visible absorbance of the dye solution after photoelectrocatalytic activity. (c) Cyclic stability of the electrode under photoelectrocatalytic activity. (d) Nyquist plot of the AD@rGO electrode and AC-CQDs electrode. (e)Raman analysis of the electrodes.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The challenges of secondary pollution and limited stability of metallic catalysts/quantum dots that are used for water treatment must be resolved in the emerging ecofriendly environmental systems. Conversely, carbon materials, specifically, conventional carbon quantum dots (C-CQDs) have emerged as an abundant, stable, and biocompatible alternative for visible-light-driven photocatalysts, that are used for water treatment. Despite these advantages, the fast charge recombination in quantum-confined systems, complex purification, and limited optoelectronic performance are bottlenecks in the practical application of C-CQDs. To address these issues, we proposed a scalable structural design of C-CQDs with enhanced photocatalytic properties. The synthesis process of CQDs was modified to yield a highly amorphous core carbon quantum dots (AC-CQDs), which was controlled by varying the synthesis temperature. The low initial temperature during the synthesis of the AC-CQDs yields an amorphous core, which provides a high electrical resistance; hence, the indirect recombination occurring through core conductivity is significantly suppressed. To ensure scalable synthesis and stability, AC-CQDs were directly grown on reduced graphene oxide, which was coated on a carbon fabric to fabricate a textile-structured electrode. Efficient charge separation in the proposed catalyst electrode structure offers significantly improved photoelectrocatalytic activity, i.e., 100% effluent dye degradation in 25 min.

ce:section.ce:para: The partial carbonization of organic precursors leads to the formation of conventional carbon quantum dots (C-CQDs), which have replaced the existing metal-based nanomaterials owing to their nontoxicity, low cost, tunable surface, and high stability [1],[2],[3],[4],[5]. The organic precursors used in C-CQD synthesis are widely available and can be converted into C-CQDs by controlled carbonization [6]. The C-CQDs are spherical particles that offer unique optoelectronic characteristics based on their obvious crystal lattice and functional groups enriched surface [7]. Hence, the properties of C-CQDs can be tuned based on the subdomain state within the graphitic carbon core, functional groups, and combined effect of both (in some cases) [7,8]. When compared to other carbonized carbon materials (activated charcoal, etc.), C-CQDs show exceptional optical (absorbance and fluorescence) and catalytic (photocatalytic and electrocatalytic) properties. Such optoelectronic properties arise from the significantly small size of C-CQDs; for example, the unique optical characteristics are due to the quantum confinement effect of excitons in a small crystal size [9]. At lower temperatures, the precursors are cross-linked via condensation polymerization, thus yielding a carbonized polymer dot (CPDs) [10]. The hybrid structure of cross-linked polymer network and carbon is observed at this stage, which can be interpreted as incompletely carbonized polymer clusters [7,10]. Similarly, a small size also provides a high surface area and surface functional groups for electrocatalytic activity [11]. Such size-dependent optoelectronic properties are commonly observed in all types of semiconductor quantum dots; however, the properties of C-CQDs can also be directly manipulated by tuning the surface functionality [12]. Surface functional groups have various energy levels, which can result in a series of emissive traps/energy levels between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) levels. These intermediate energy levels formed as a result of surface functional groups are called surface states. Importantly, surface states only consist of surface functional groups attached directly to the hybridized carbon crystallites on the surface (as explained in the subsequent section) [12]. A higher degree of surface functionality generates more surface defects or surface states, resulting in red-shifted optical absorbance and associated fluorescence and photocatalysis [12]. Similarly, surface functional groups serve as active centers for charge transfer reactions in electrocatalysis, which indirectly improves the electrocatalytic activity [13]. Previous studies have focused on improving the photocatalytic activity, fluorescence, and electrocatalytic activity of C-CQDs using surface states [14]. However, the modulation of the properties of C-CQDs by tuning the crystal structure is a rarely explored area of research. Consequently, we tested the role of the C-CQD crystal structure in tuning the optoelectronic properties.

The evolution of the crystal structure during the formation of C-CQDs depends on the synthesis temperature and time, as the both variables control the degree of carbonization [15,16]. The carbonization of C-CQDs was studied by Rogach et al., and they determined that low-temperature carbonization led to the formation of fluorophores―fluorescent conjugated molecules, produced as a result of the condensation reaction of precursors [17]. Upon extending the carbonization time at low temperatures, amorphous (sp3-hybridized) C-CQD structures are formed by the crosslinking of fluorophores (Fig. 1 (a)) [17]. Conversely, The high-temperature carbonization leads to the formation of a completely sp2-hybridized carbon structure, which lacks surface states (functional groups), molecular states, and defects states [17]. The absence of any florescent center results in the non-emissive nature of such a carbon structure. The fluorophores -colorful molecules formed by the polymerization of organic precursors- are responsible for the blue emission, which are originated from molecular states (tested in next section) [17,18]. Considering the limitations of completely carbonized structures, an intermediate carbonization temperature is ideal to achieve a partially carbonized C-CQD structure, having both sp3- and sp2-carbonized subdomains [19]. The initial thermal treatment causes the condensation of the precursors, thus making conjugated molecules on the surface of CPDs [10]. These surface molecules generate the molecular states, which serve as the emission center for fluorescence. The extended heat treatment causes the partial carbonization of these molecules, which serves as a functional group and tunes the band structure in the form of surface states. Surface states introduce new energy levels between HOMO-LUMO level, thus causing extended visible light absorbance and larger Stokes Shift [8]. As illustrated in Fig. 1(a), at a suitable carbonization temperature, a polycrystalline structure of C-CQDs is formed, in which sp2-crystalline domains are dispersed in an amorphous skeleton. The optoelectronic properties of C-CQDs are determined by the size and degree of hybridization in these sp2-crystalline domains [19]. By varying the size of the individual crystallites or crystalline domains, the band alignment can also be varied. Using density functional theory calculations, it was proved that the HOMO–LUMO level varies by changing the size and crystallinity of subdomains [19].

In C-CQD structure, the sp2-crystalline domains on the edges play a critical role in the optoelectronic properties as they are directly crosslinked with the surface functional groups, that are responsible for surface states [12]. A higher degree of surface states increases the radiative and high Stokes shift recombination [20]. By contrast, the core crystallites are responsible for nonradiative and fast recombination of excitons, as shown in Fig. 1(b) [20]. If the core structure of C-CQDs is synthesized with an amorphous nature, the indirect recombination can be suppressed effectively owing to the lower conductivity of the core. In addition to this phenomenological effect, an effective Z-scheme structure can be formed by the gradient of crystallinity from the core to the edge [19]. The possible Z-scheme structure expected for amorphous-core carbon quantum dots (AC-CQDs) is shown in Fig. 1(b). In such an aligned band structure, the fast recombination of electrons is expected to be suppressed. A higher lifetime of excitons and their efficient separation are useful for enhancing the photocatalytic activity. With this motivation, we designed an AC-CQD-based electrode for photocatalytic dye degradation from water.

Considering the secondary pollution caused by the nanomaterials themselves, we fabricated a porous electrode using carbon cloth (CC) as a textile substrate. Reduced graphene oxide (rGO) nanosheets were used as an intermediate to load quantum dots on the CC. The graphene oxide sheets were added to the solution used for the synthesis of quantum dots, which allowed the direct growth of quantum dots on the surface of graphene oxide nanosheets. During the synthesis of quantum dots, graphene sheets were also partially reduced to form rGO sheets. The AC-CQDs were synthesized using gradient heating, i.e., low temperature for initial carbonization and high temperature for final carbonization (Fig. 2 ). The rGO sheets with AC-CQDs grown on their surfaces are labeled as AD@rGO, which were then loaded onto the CC. A similar process was used for the synthesis of C-CQDs, except that it involved uniform heating at 180 °C, and their composite with rGO is referred to as CD@rGO (Fig. 2). Both AD@rGO- and CD@rGO-loaded CCs were tested for the photoelectrocatalytic degradation of methylene blue from water. When compared to CD@rGO, the AD@rGO electrode showed a significantly improved degradation percentage, which was associated with better charge separation in the AC-CQDs. The mechanism of charge separation was elucidated using various electrocatalytic and optical techniques. When compared to the mixed-phase amorphous and sp2-crystalline domains in the C-CQDs, separate amorphous and sp2-crystalline domains were observed in AC-CQDs. It is important to note that, for referring to both C-CQDs and AD-CQDs, only quantum dot- or carbon quantum dot (CQD)-related terms are used.

For the synthesis of rGO, graphite powder with a particle size less than 100 μm (Asbury Carbons, USA) was used as the starting material. Other chemicals, including sulfuric acid (99%), KMnO4, H2O2 (35%), HCl (37%), and NaBH4 were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. For the bottom-up synthesis of CQDs, citric acid and ethylenediamine were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Common solvents and laboratory chemicals were purchased from a local market. Woven CC (W0S1011) with an aerial density of 125 g/m2 was purchased from NARA Cell-Tech Corp (South Korea). All chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Deionized (DI) water and thoroughly washed glassware were used throughout the experiment.

To study the electrocatalytic properties of CQDs (of both types), conductive nano-support is compulsory as pure CQD films have high resistance. The conductive support provided efficient channels for charge transport during electrocatalytic and photocatalytic reactions. For this purpose, we used rGO as a nano-support for the growth of CQDs. Graphene oxide was synthesized from graphite powder and CQDs were directly grown on rGO by introducing graphene oxide sheets during the synthesis of the CQDs. The CQD-modified rGO was then coated on a CC to develop a flexible electrode and its electrocatalytic, photocatalytic, and photoelectrocatalytic activities were tested. However, for studying the optical properties, the CQD colloidal solutions (without rGO) were considered. To synthesize CQDs with different structures, we used variations in the carbonization temperature. An overall summary of the experimental design is shown in Fig. 2.

Graphene oxide sheets were synthesized from graphite powder using a modified Hummers' method as reported in Refs. [14,15]. Initially, graphite (5 g) was dispersed in H2SO4 (250 mL) under vigorous stirring. The graphite dispersion was maintained in an ice bath to lower the temperature to less than 5 °C. While maintaining the low temperature and stirring at a high speed, KMnO4 (25 g) was added slowly. Care must be taken at this step as a spontaneous exothermic reaction occurs at this stage; this is the reason for maintaining the temperature at less than 5 °C. To achieve an oxidation reaction, the temperature of the solution was increased up to 35 °C for 8 h to obtain graphite oxide. After the completion of oxidation, the solution was again transferred to an ice bath to stop the oxidation reaction. While maintaining the temperature at less than 5 °C, 2 L of DI water was added dropwise. Finally, to convert the graphite oxide to graphene, 5 mL of H2O2 was added, until the solution color was changed to bright yellow. Then, graphene oxide dispersion was washed with HCl, acetone, and DI water to remove metallic, organic, and unreacted impurities, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of conventional carbon quantum dots (C-CQDs) on rGO
ce:section.ce:para: The inspiration of CQDs@rGO composite fabrication was from reference [2], whereas instead of post sensitization, we used one-step direct growth. Typically, 3 g of citric acid, 10 ml of NH3, and 2.55 ml of ethylenediamine were dissolved in 100 ml of 1% graphene oxide solution. Graphene oxide sheets were dispersed in an ethanol and water (1:1) mixture using water bath sonication and magnetic stirring. This solution of graphene oxide and CQD precursors was treated at 180 °C in a 220 mL capacity Teflon-lined hydrothermal chamber for 6 h. After cooling to room temperature, the CDs@rGO composite structure was separated using filter paper (0.2 μm). The C-CQDs were collected from the filtrate, purified via centrifugation, and collected using a freeze dryer. It is important to note that the filtered CD@rGO cake was also freeze-dried to avoid irreversible aggregation in the rGO sheets. It is important to note that to get a dense growth on GO, the heating rate must be slow. For example, to reach temperature of 180 °C, the heating rate should be 3 °C/min. In addition, if commercial GO is being used, the etching of 2% GO solution in 10 wt% H2O2 assists in heterogeneous growth on GO sheets. The etching treatment generates active sites on GO sheets, which serve as nucleation site for the growth of CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of amorphous-core carbon quantum dots (AC-CQDs) on rGO
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis processes of AC-CQDs and AD@rGO were similar to those of C-CQDs and CD@rGO, except for the incorporation of gradient heating temperature during the hydrothermal treatment. The initial temperature of the hydrothermal chamber was maintained at 140 °C for 3 h; then, the temperature was increased to 200 °C for 3 h. After cooling, a similar separation and purification method was used for obtaining the AC@rGO composite structure.

The freeze-dried composite structure was loaded onto the CC after washing the CC thoroughly with nitric acid. Acid treatment not only removes the surface impurities but also increases the adsorption capability of the CC for attaching rGO sheets to its surface. The freeze-dried composite was dispersed in water to make a 2% aqueous solution, which was then loaded onto the CC via the dip-dry method. Four dip-dry cycles were sufficient to completely cover the CC surface with the CQDs-modified rGO composite structure. After loading on the CC, the electrode was treated with NaBH4 solution for the complete reduction of rGO. Further, the NaBH4 treatment helped to boost the catalytic activity of CQDs (as passivating ethylene diamine chains are removed from the surface). After the reduction process, the electrode was washed and analyzed.

The structures of the CQDs and associated rGO composites were observed at high resolution using a transmission electron microscope (JEOL JEM-2100F). Furthermore, the surface morphologies of the composite structure and electrode were analyzed using a field-emission scanning electron microscope (JEOL JSM-6700F). The crystal structure was analyzed via wide-angle X-ray diffraction (WAXD) using a Rigaku Denki X-ray generator (Rigaku, D/Max-2500) equipped with CuKα radiation (λ = 1.54181 Å) at 40 kV and 60 mA. WAXD data were collected with a step interval of 0.02° and at a speed of 5° min−1 in the 2-theta range of 10–80°. Structural defects were further probed using a Raman spectrometer (NWS-3100, JASCO, Japan), which is equipped with an excitation wavelength of 532 nm. The composition of the composite structure was further studied via high-resolution X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; multiscale ESCA.200 system). An X-ray source of AlKα was used with an energy step size of 0.05 eV. To investigate the electrocatalytic properties of the composite structure, cyclic voltammetry (CV) experiments were conducted using a three-electrode system in an argon-purged electrolyte (0.01 M LiClO4, 10 mM LiI, and 1 mM I2 prepared in acetonitrile solution). The tests were performed at a scan rate of 5 mVs−1 with an ultimate electrochemical workstation (BioLogic Company). The HOMO–LUMO level of the CQDs was calculated from the CV measurements in 0.1 M tetrabutylammonium hexafluorophosphate in tetrahydrofuran electrolyte. Three-electrode assemblies, i.e., a graphite electrode, Ag/AgCl electrode, and Pt wire, were used as the working, counter, and reference electrodes, respectively. Moreover, ferrocene (5 mM) was used as an external reference to calibrate the HOMO–LUMO level. All tests were performed at a scan rate of 40 mV/s.

The photocatalytic activity of the CQDs was tested for the electrode form, which was tested by immersing it in the dye solution and exposing it to a simulated solar spectrum (1 sun). Initially, the samples were immersed in organic dye-contaminated water under dark conditions (to exclude the adsorption effect). After reaching the saturation capacity in adsorption, the concentration of the dye was considered as the reference concentration. The samples were then exposed to a light source for the degradation of contaminants. The variation in the concentration of methylene was recorded every 20 min using an ultraviolet (UV)-visible spectrometer (Shimadzu Co., Kyoto, Japan). Photocatalysis testing was performed under continuous magnetic stirring to ensure that the rate of degradation was not diffusion limited. Similarly, an oil bath was used to maintain the temperature of the degradation bath to avoid the evaporation of water vapor owing to the heating caused by the simulated sunlight. To test the degradation of the photoelectrocatalytic dye, an additional bias voltage was applied to the composite electrode. For optical characterization, the fluorescence spectrum was recorded using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (SCINCO, South Korea). The conductivity of CQDs colloids was tested using liquid electrical conductivity meter CC-511.

CQDs are formed by the controlled partial carbonization of the carbon source, where the degree of carbonization defines the overall optoelectronic properties. Therefore, to achieve different core structures, we modified the synthesis conditions, particularly the carbonization temperature of the CQDs, as shown in Fig. 2(a). Typically, the synthesis of CQDs on rGO sheets is self-driven via the strong hydrogen bonding between the functional groups of graphene oxide and the precursors of CQDs, as shown in Fig. 2(b). Upon hydrothermal treatment, the condensation reaction of precursors to fluorophores, i.e., intermediate fluorescent molecules, occurs. During this condensation reaction, some of the CQDs are also directly crosslinked with the functional groups of graphene oxide. Thermal treatment also causes a partial reduction of the graphene oxide sheets as the reaction proceeds. Finally, upon extended carbonization, thermal reduction of graphene oxide occurs, along with the carbonization of the fluorophores to the CQDs (Fig. 2(b)).

To confirm this heterogeneous growth of CQDs on the rGO surface, we analyzed the CQD-modified rGO sheets via transmission electron microscopy (TEM). A similar structure was expected for the homogeneously synthesized CQDs, i.e., those formed in the solution. The commonly used hydrothermal carbonization (at constant temperature) leads to the formation of partially crystalline and partially amorphous structures of C-CQDs, as shown schematically and through the TEM image (Fig. 3 (a)) [21]. In contrast, the two phases of carbon domains can be observed in the AD@rGO structure (schematic illustration and TEM image shown in Fig. 3(b)). This bi-phase structure confirms that the AC-CQDs are formed with a difference in crystallinity between the core and shell due to the temperature difference, which is as we expected. When compared to the bare rGO sheets (Fig. 3(c)), the growth of C-CQDs can be observed on rGO at different resolutions, as shown in Fig. 3(d–f). At lower resolutions, the CD@rGO composite revealed larger particles; however, at high resolution (Fig. 3(e)), it can be observed that the C-CQDs form small colonies. The average size distribution of C-CQDs calculated from the analysis of TEM images showed variations ranging from 2 to 12 nm (Fig. S1(a)), with an average size of 5 nm. The C-CQD structure showed some crystallites on the surface/edge, whereas some crystallites existed in the core, exhibiting a mixed-phase growth (Fig. 3(f)). The surface functional groups that are hybridized with the edge crystallites are predominantly responsible for the surface states, which are essential for enhanced fluorescent properties. In contrast, the core crystallites serve as a source of leakage/indirect recombination for excited electrons. If the core is completely amorphous, the conductivity of the core can be significantly reduced, thereby suppressing fast charge recombinations. Slower recombinations provide a longer lifetime for the excited electrons, thereby producing a higher content of reactive oxygen species, which is responsible for photocatalytic activity [2,22–24]. Therefore, AC-CQDs were synthesized by maintaining the initial temperature of synthesis at a low value (140 °C) and final carbonization temperature was high. Hence, the core-shell type structure that is formed because of the temperature gradient, as highlighted in Fig. 3(b). In addition to the core-shell structure, a side-by-side arrangement of crystalline and amorphous domains was also observed in some cases, as shown in Fig. 3(h and i). The size distribution of AC-CQDs calculated from the TEM analysis varied between 2 and 12 nm (Fig. S1(b)), with an average size of 4 nm. In such an AD@rGO composite structure, amorphous carbon is connected to the rGO on one side and sp2-crystalline domains on the other side. We expect that although the amorphous domain is not present in the core, it can still reduce the fast recombinations by resulting in a Z-scheme band alignment (which is discussed in the subsequent section). Moreover, as tested by other researchers, the integration of CQDs with rGO further reduces the charge recombination due to the higher electron mobility provided by rGO [25,26]. Similarly, other carbon derivatives; like carbon nano-tubes (CNTs) are also employed extensively to increase the charge mobility [27,28]. Additional TEM images are provided in the end of supporting information and result and mechanism discussion section.

Previously, rGO and CQD composites were synthesized by the post-crosslinking of CQDs via thermal annealing in a hydrothermal process [2]. For such composite fabrication, a multistep process is required, such as the synthesis of CQDs, purification using a dialysis membrane, activation of rGO/CQDs using a coupling agent, and thermal annealing. Particularly, the dialysis membrane-based separation is a time-consuming process, whereas repeated heating for synthesis and crosslinking is an energy-consuming step. Despite all the efforts, the stability of such post-crosslinked systems is low; hence, it can only be used for five cycles. In contrast, our process has only one step and is facile; moreover, as the production yield of our synthesis reaction is nearly 100%, direct heterogeneous growth of CQDs is possible. During synthesis, the graphene sheets serve as a passivating agent that restricts the growth of quantum dots to a larger scale. Our proposed one-step heterogeneous growth of CQDs is also expected to provide a strong interface for the crosslinking of CQDs with rGO. Hence, the leaching off of the quantum dots from the composite (AD/CD@rGO) during the treatment process would be significantly less than that in previously reported systems [2].

To confirm the synthesis of quantum dots on rGO, we probed the composite structures using various characterization methods. To analyze the differences in surface morphology, we performed scanning electron microscopy (SEM) at different developmental stages of the electrode (Fig. 4 (a–d)). First, we analyzed the bare CC, which served as a substrate for electrode fabrication (Fig. 4(a)). A fibrous structure was observed in the CC, with an average diameter of 5–7 μm for each fiber. Among all textile fibers, carbon fiber has the finest structure, which provides a high surface area for the loading of nanocatalysts. Owing to its conductive nature and superior corrosion resistance, the CC is the first choice as a substrate material in water treatment applications [24]. The adsorption of rGO sheets on the CC was observed after the first coating, where the rGO sheets covered the individual fibers (Fig. 4(b)). Repeated loading cycles caused the formation of a uniform film, as shown in Fig. 4(b). Analysis of the bare rGO sheet at high resolution shows a relatively uniform surface morphology for the bare rGO sheets (Fig. S1(c)). When compared to the smooth 2D planar morphology of the bare rGO sheet, the quantum-dot-modified rGO showed the appearance of wrinkles (Figs. S1(d and e)). The high-resolution analysis showed the formation of a crumpled morphology in the CD@rGO and AD@rGO, as shown in Fig. 4(c and d), respectively. Owing to their ultra-small size, the growth of individual quantum dots cannot be observed via SEM. Both the quantum-dot-modified rGO samples showed a similar wrinkled morphology.

Furthermore, the crystal structures of the CQDs and rGO composites were analyzed using WAXD, as shown in Fig. 4(e). The WAXD analysis revealed two peaks located around 24 and 43°, which originate from the 002 and 100 graphitic planes of carbon crystallites in the quantum-dot-modified rGO, respectively. The WAXD results confirm that the 002 plane exhibits a dominant growth during the formation of CQDs, as is commonly observed [12,29,30]. Marginal differences in the peak position in the 002 plane (inset of Fig. 4(e)) and intensity were observed, which were caused by the evolution of crystallites at different temperatures. The lower diffraction angle and lower peak intensity are due to a higher content of amorphous skeleton and smaller-sized crystalline domain formation, respectively. For instance, the CD@rGO composite showed a lower peak intensity and the lowest angle of diffraction. This effect is due to the formation of the amorphous skeleton and sp2-crystalline domains in parallel (mixed-phase structure) while using an intermediate carbonization temperature of 180 °C. In contrast, the amorphous and sp2-crystalline domains are formed separately in AC-CQDs because of the gradient carbonization temperature. Because of this, sp2-crystalline domains formed (specifically at high temperature) have a larger size; hence, a higher-intensity peak was observed in the 002 plane [12,29,30]. Moreover, an additional low-intensity peak around 13° was observed for AC-CQDs, which can be associated with the formation of highly oxidized and disordered amorphous carbon [31].

The elemental compositions of the samples were analyzed via XPS, as shown in Fig. 4(f). Both the AD@rGO- and CD@rGO-based electrodes showed similar constituent elements, i.e., carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen, at binding energies of 285, 400, and 532 eV, respectively. The XPS spectra of both samples (Fig. 4(f)) highlight the concentrations of nitrogen and oxygen attached to the carbon skeleton. Differences in the concentration and structure of oxygen were observed (Fig. 4(f)), whereas the nitrogen and carbon contents were almost similar (Fig. S1(f)). The deconvoluted O 1s spectrum (Fig. 4(g)) shows two peaks at binding energies of 531.2, and 532.8 eV which can be associated with the C=O and C–O, bonds, respectively. When compared to CD@rGO, the AD@rGO composite revealed a 25% higher content of C–O bonds (Fig. 4(g)). This can be attributed to the highly amorphous and oxidized carbon that is formed at a lower temperature [32]. As the initial temperature is low, the complete removal of oxygen during the initial condensation reaction is not favored; hence, the amorphous carbon part formed at a lower temperature has a higher oxygen content. As nitrogen induces the CQD skeleton at a much later stage, both AD@rGO and CD@rGO showed similar nitrogen structures [30].

In conclusion, the structural characterizations prove that the amorphous-core (or bi-phase) structure of AC-CQDs was formed by gradient heating during carbonization. A significantly higher content of amorphous carbon, which was enriched with oxygenated functional groups, was observed in the AC-CQDs. In contrast, uniform heating induced the formation of mixed (amorphous and crystalline) phases in the C-CQDs. The in situ growth of CQDs on rGO was also confirmed for both CQD structures.

Optical characterization of the quantum dot colloid was performed to approximate the band structures of both AC-CQDs and C-CQDs. The fluorescence and related optical characteristics of CQDs are completely quenched after coupling with rGO; therefore, instead of the composite structure, only the CQD colloid was tested for optical characterization. The UV–visible absorption spectrum of the CQDs is shown in Fig. 5 (a). Both CQDs show a similar absorbance range, with a marginal variation in the absorbance intensities. Typically, the absorbance of both CQDs can be divided into two main regions: (1) π → π * and (2) n → π * transitions related to absorbance below and above 300 nm, respectively. Excitations in the high-energy spectrum, i.e., below 300 nm, originate from the carbon cores; hence, these characteristic peaks are observed in both AC-CQDs and C-CQDs [33]. A marginally broad and higher-intensity peak for the n → π* and π → π* transitions of C-CQDs were observed, when compared to AC-CQDs. This optical behavior can be attributed to the higher content of pyridinic nitrogen in the core, which facilitates electron transitions by donating extra electrons in the carbon crystallites [34]. A higher density of electrons assists in radiative and direct recombination. Moreover, a shouldered peak of extended visible light absorbance was observed in both samples, in the range of 400–500 nm, as shown in the high-resolution inset in Fig. 5(a). This red-shifted absorption is related to the surface states, induced by the non-bonding orbitals of surface functional groups and heteroatom doping [35]. Owing to the surface states, band-edge transitions at lower energies are possible in the visible spectrum. The higher content of the functional groups narrows the HOMO–LUMO energy gap, allowing transitions at lower energies [36]. This visible light absorbance is responsible for the photocatalytic activity under visible light in both types of CQDs.

In addition to the absorbance spectrum, another parameter that critically affects the photocatalytic activity is the lifetime of the excited electron–hole pair. The photocatalytic activity of nanomaterials is strongly influenced by efficient charge separation. As the lifetime of the photoexcited electron–hole pair increases, the production of reactive oxygen species increases, which is essential for the photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants. To test the effectiveness of the bi-phase-structured AC-CQDs for enhancing the lifetime, time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) was tested, as shown in Fig. 5(b). The TRPL curves of both samples can be fitted using a tri-exponential function, which shows multiple (radiative and nonradiative) channels in CQDs. The C-CQDs showed values of 10.4, 0.28, and 2.1 ns for the τ1, τ2, and τ3, respectively. In contrast, the τ1, τ2, and τ3 values for the AC-CQDs were observed to be 3.15, 0.35, and 10.3 ns, respectively. The average lifetime calculated for the C-CQDs and AC-CQDs was 0.28 and 0.78 ns, respectively, as tabulated in Fig. 5(b). Initially, slow recombinations were observed for the AC-CQDs, as indicated by the arrow in the TRPL graph (Fig. 5(b)). These initial slow recombinations are responsible for the overall 1.78 times higher value of lifetime in the AC-CQDs, when compared to that of C-CQDs. This significantly higher lifetime could be attributed to the Z-scheme alignment formed between the amorphous core and the crystalline shell. Moreover, the lower conductivity of the core benefits the slower recombinations, as the leakage current through the core can be suppressed. This effect was further confirmed by measuring the conductivity of CQDs’ colloid, with a concentration of 0.5 wt%. AC-CQDs and C-CQDs colloids showed conductivity of 1.991 and 2.560 mS/cm, respectively.

To confirm the Z-scheme alignment, we analyzed the difference in the HOMO–LUMO energy level of the structure formed at different carbonization temperatures. Ferrocene was used as an external reference system for calibration (Fig. S2(a)). The band structures of the CQDs synthesized for 3 h each at low (140 °C) and high (200 °C) temperatures are shown in Fig. S2(b). Amorphous and sp2-crystalline domains are expected to be formed in such CQDs, and the HOMO–LUMO levels of the crystalline and amorphous CQD domains are shown in Fig. S2(b). The HOMO–LUMO levels of both domains were calculated using a cyclic voltammogram according to the equation shown in Fig. S2(a). Finally, from the HOMO–LUMO levels of both structures, a possible electron–hole separation can be expected in AC-CQDs, as illustrated in Fig. S2(c). The crystalline and amorphous domains will be hole-rich and electron-rich, respectively; hence, higher charge separation is possible in the gradient temperature-synthesized AC-CQDs, as both crystalline and amorphous domains form a junction. In general, the CQDs with single path (mono-exponential decay fit) recombinations show a higher value of TRPL, In general, the CQDs with single path (mono-exponential decay fit) recombinations show a higher value of TRPL, which is due to higher stability of excitons when there is a single path available for recombination. Recombination is relatively fast when multiple channels are available for recombination, as observed in multi-exponential decay curves [37]. On the other hand, in our case of the bi-phase synthesis of the AC-CQD structure resulted in a longer lifetime [37].

Fluorescence is a characteristic property of CQDs, which can be directly related to their electronic structure. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra confirm the quantum confinement effect and the associated photoexcitation in CQDs. The PL of the CQDs (Fig. 5 (c, d)) showed a similar Stokes shift, i.e., the bluish-green emission of both CQDs was centered at 525 nm. In contrast, a significant difference in the emission intensity was observed, i.e., the C-CQDs showed significantly higher PL intensity than the AC-CQDs. With respect to the Stokes shift, excitation-dependent emission was observed, which is a feature of surface states-related emission [14]. In such a case, the probability of electronic transitions is strongly influenced by the surface functional groups, as compared to the quantum confinement effect [14]. Compared to AC-CQDs, fewer defects exist in the core of C-CQDs. A high density of functional groups is present on the edge sites of C-CQDs, with a relatively lower concentration of defects in the core [38]. Specifically, aminated edge functional groups play a critical role in the case of N-doped CQDs, as they contribute to an increase in electron density near Fermi level, which favors radiative recombinations [39,40]. The difference in PL intensity was quantified through quantum yield (QY) measurements. The QY of the CQDs was calculated using a method as described in previous studies [41,42]. Briefly, the area under the curve for the PL intensity was integrated for C-CQDs, AC-CQDs, and the reference Rhodamine B dye. This integrated sum was plotted against the absorbance value to obtain a straight line with a slope M (optical density), which was then used to calculate the QY according to the following equation:
(1)
P L − Q Y s = P L − Q Y r .

Where the subscripts “r” and “s” denote the reference (dye) and CQDs, respectively. “
η
” denotes the refractive index of the solvents that are used, which was DI water for all samples. All tested solutions were of equal concentrations, while the absorbance of colloidal solutions must be below 0.1% to avoid the concentration-dependent self-reabsorption of CQD emissions.

Using this method, the QY of the C-CQDs was determined to be 52%, whereas that of the AC-CQDs was 12%. The lower QY of the AC-CQD emission can be regarded to be caused by a lower quantum confinement effect, which favors nonradiative or indirect recombinations. A lower QY can also be related to better charge channeling, resulting in better charge separation, as observed in catalysts with S/Z-scheme junctions [43]. In this case, the lower QY is associated with lower quantum confinement of the excitons [43]. Furthermore, the difference in PL can also be observed by placing the samples under a UV lamp, as shown in Fig. S3. A significant difference in the emission intensity in both the colloidal solutions of 0.1% concentration was observed (Fig. S3). In general, the CQDs with high crystallinity shows lower QY; however, in the case of C-CQDs, the QY is higher, as compared to AC-CQDs. This was related to higher radiative recombinations in C-CQDs, as obvious from the significantly higher value of τ1 in TRPL, which is related to radiative recombinations. In contrast, the recombinations occurring in AC-CQDs dominantly follow slow non-radiative pathways, associated with higher values of τ2, and τ3. Despite having higher functional groups, the AC-CQDs showed nonradiative recombinations, which might be due to charge channeling in the p-n junction type structure.

The photocatalytic activity of CQDs is an interesting area of application for the utilization of photoexcited electrons. The photocatalytic activity of CQDs competes with other semiconductors based on their high corrosion resistance, biocompatibility, and visible light activity [44,45]. We tested the effect of our designed structures on the photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue dye under visible light. Based on the suitable photoexcitation spectrum and lifetime of the excited electrons confirmed from the UV–visible absorbance and TRPL, we expected the potential occurrence of photoactivity in CQDs. To compare the photocatalytic activity of the CQDs, the equal mass loading of AD@rGO and CD@rGO composites on the CC (Fig. 4(b–d)) was tested, i.e., electrode form analysis was performed. The conductive support of rGO and carbon fiber provides electron mobility, thereby suppressing fast charge recombination. Moreover, by using this electrode, the process step for centrifugation or filtration of nanomaterials from treated water can be avoided. Although the immobilized photocatalyst (in electrode form) exhibits significantly lower activity than in the mobile phase (in powder form), the immobilized form is closer to practical applications. Also, the photocatalytic activity of undoped and N-doped CQDs is well understood before. The nitrogen doping favors the superior photocatalysis of CQDs by creating the surface charge redistribution, effective bandgap tuning for extending visible light absorbance, and suitable junction formation [24]. In contrast, undoped-CQDs can only absorb UV light, hence their visible light photocatalysis is not possible. In order to achieve the visible light photo-catalysis, nitrogen doping is important and the best performance was achieved with nitrogen doping of 2.5% [46].

The photocatalytic activities of the different electrodes are shown in Fig. 6 (a). Bare rGO showed (only) the adsorption of the dye and the photocatalytic activity was negligible. Comparatively, the photoactivity of the CQD-modified rGO was significantly higher, which can be attributed to its higher surface area. However, the photocatalytic degradation of AD@rGO was the highest, i.e., 100% degradation after 40 min (Fig. 6(a)). In contrast, CD@rGO showed 70% degradation after 70 min. Although AC-CQDs have a lower absorbance intensity, the longer lifetime of the excitons results in superior photodegradation of the dye. The UV–visible absorbance peak of methylene blue dye after 10 min of treatment is shown in Fig. 6(b). It can be observed that AD@rGO showed a skewed dye spectrum. Such variation in shape shows that the AC-CQDs rapidly converted the dye molecules to intermediates within 10 min. Furthermore, we also tested the repeated photocatalytic degradation by the AD@rGO electrode, which showed negligible variation (∼4% decrease) in performance even after five cycles of photocatalysis. Comparatively, the previous rGO-modified CQD electrodes have limited stability owing to the removal of CQDs during photocatalytic treatment [2,47]. Hence, to regenerate the rGO electrodes, repeated CQD sensitization is required. The better stability of our composite structure may be because of its direct growth. Direct growth provides an overlapped interface between the rGO and the CQDs; hence, the peeling off of CQDs is suppressed, when compared to thermally crosslinked or adsorbed CQD systems. It is important to note that during the structural evolution of CQDs, heating time, temperature, and concentration of precursors are interdependent, as reported in previous work [30]. In this work we optimized the concentration of precursors, to control the heterogeneous growth and size of CQDs. The concentration of reactants and reaction time were optimized to achieve controlled growth. Different concentration of citric acid changes the size and concentration of C-CQDs grown on rGO, which affects the photocatalytic activity of the composite structure (Fig. S4). At low concentrations, the heterogeneous growth of CQDs is negligible, thus photocatalytic activity is also almost negligible (Figs. S4(a and b)). Higher concentrations cause the aggregation of CQDs to carbon nanoparticles, which offer limited surface area and photocatalytic activity (Figs. S4(b and c)). Whereas, the suitable size of C-CQDs was synthesized at a concentration of 1 g, which was considered optimized for this work.

The electrocatalytic activities of the CQDs and composite electrodes were tested to further probe the electron dynamics under the applied voltage. The electrocatalytic activity of the CQDs can be approximated based on the CV of the electrode in an iodide electrolyte system. The electrocatalytic activity curves (Fig. 6(c)) of the CQDs showed poor electrocatalytic activity, with only a low-intensity oxidation peak. This is because of the poor conductivity of the CQDs. In contrast, the coupling of rGO showed a significant improvement in both oxidation and reduction peaks, with positive and negative current densities, respectively. The oxidation and reduction reactions occurring on the electrode surface are shown in Fig. 6(d). The superior electrocatalytic activity can be evaluated on the basis of a higher current density and lower peak-to-peak potential (EPP, i.e., the potential difference between the oxidation and reduction peaks). The electrocatalytic curves of both CQDs showed a significant difference in current density and minor variation in EPP, when compared to that of bare rGO. The inset table of Fig. 6(d) summarizes the oxidation-current density (IOX), which exhibits the highest value for AD@rGO. Conversely, when compared to bare rGO, AD@rGO showed an almost negligible difference in reduction current (IRED) density. Conversely, CD@rGO showed a marginal improvement in both IOX and IRED.

Without the rGO substrate, the charge transfer reaction of the CQDs required high potentials. The extremely small size of CQDs creates a strong confinement of charge, which favors recombination instead of charge transfer. Owing to this effect, the photocatalytic activity of pure CQDs is poor [48]. Moreover, the recovery of CQDs from treated water requires highly complex processing techniques, which limits the scalability of pure CQD catalysts [48]. In contrast, the coupling of nanosubstrates (such as rGO) provides suitable electron mobility while providing charge carrier paths [49]. A similar effect was induced by the CC substrate, which also enhanced the charge mobility owing to its intrinsic conductive nature. The synergistic combination of CQDs with rGO also enhanced the performance of bare rGO, as shown in Fig. 6(d). An increase in current density is related to the higher surface area provided by the small size of the grown CQDs [50,51]. Moreover, the CQDs serve as a spacer to avoid the restacking of rGO sheets; hence, the complex diffusion of the electrolyte through the stacked rGO structure can be avoided [23]. CQDs provide a defect-rich surface, which serves as an active site for electrochemical reactions [23]. Conversely, AC-CQDs are attached to the rGO through the amorphous domains, which have high series resistance; therefore, the improvement in IRED is almost negligible, as electron flow from the rGO to AC-CQDs is restricted. Conversely, the increase in IOX shows that the AC-CQDs are active under positive voltages, with respect to hole transport. This confirms that the AC-CQDs are connected to rGO through a p-n junction, which favors hole accumulation toward the AC-CQDs, whereas electron flow is favored toward the rGO.

Based on both the photocatalytic and electrocatalytic activities of the electrode, we tested the electrode for photoelectrocatalytic activity. For this purpose, we evaluated dye degradation under the combined application of voltage and light sources. The biased voltage range for photoelectrocatalysis and the flat band potential of both electrodes were tested using the Mott–Schottky plot (Fig. 7 (a)). The negative slope of the curve ranging from −1 to 1 V shows that the composite structure has p-type characteristics. The flat band potential V F is the x-intercept of the Mott–Schottky plot, after which band bending starts. The V F of the AD@rGO and CD@rGO was 0.25 and 0.49 V, as shown in Fig. 7(a). The gradient structure showed a significantly low value of V F , which could be related to junction formation in CQDs, as reported previously [52]. Typically, highly oxygenated domains show p-type characteristics, whereas nitrogen-rich domains have n-type characteristics [52]. Gradient heating also favors the formation of oxygen-rich domains (at low temperature) and nitrogenated domains (at high temperatures); hence, a lower V F was observed for AD@rGO. Based on the higher photocatalytic activity and lower V F , AD@rGO can be considered to be more suitable for photoelectrocatalytic activity characterization. Therefore, further experiments were conducted on only the AD@rGO electrode.

The photoelectrocatalytic activity of the AD@rGO electrode was tested under both light and applied voltage. The UV–visible absorbance of the dye degradation after 25 min of photoelectrocatalysis, tested under biased voltages of 0.15, 0.25, and 0.35 V is illustrated in Fig. 7(b). The AD@rGO showed 70% degradation of dye in 25 min at 0 V, whereas at voltages of 0.15, 0.25, and 0.35 V the degradation percentages were 80, 82, and 100%, respectively. The effective voltage for photoelectrocatalysis was marginally higher than that for V F , which could be due to the energy losses in the substrate. A 100% degradation of the dye was observed in 25 min, and a significant difference in the photoelectrocatalytic activity was observed, i.e., the complete dye degradation time was reduced from 40 to 25 min. The dye degradation can also be observed from the inset images of the reference (initial) dye solution and after complete degradation. Moreover, this photoelectrocatalytic activity was retained for four consecutive cycles, with a 2% decrease in the final cycle (Fig. 7(c)). From this, the suitable stability of the electrode for repeated cycles can be justified. A suitably high performance, along with facile processing was achieved using the proposed catalyst. Comparatively, 93% degradation in 120 min of visible light for MB dye using N-doped CQDs [46]. CQDs composite with carbon-nitride showed 100% phenol degradation in 200 min under visible light [53]. The same composite structure revealed 100% degradation in110 min for RhB dye, under visible light [54]. CQDs sensitized rGO foam showed complete Cr(VI) reduction in 40 min under UV light; however, the cyclic stability was low [2].

To gain an insight into the mechanism of the electrophotocatalytic activity of the composite electrode, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and Raman analysis was performed. Specifically, the electron flow dynamics of the CQD-modified rGO electrode were studied to understand the electrochemical redox reactions of the CQDs using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. The Nyquist plot obtained from electrochemical spectroscopy is shown in Fig. 7(d), which shows one semicircle each for the electrode and CQD-coated CC. The x-intercept of the semicircle provides the value for the series resistance (RS), and the height of the semicircle refers to the charge transfer resistance (RCT). Lower resistance is considered advantageous for catalytic reactions, as the energy loss is lower in such systems. The RS is primarily caused by the resistance of substrates; therefore, a negligible difference in the series resistance was observed, as the same substrate (CC) was used for both samples. The RCT was calculated using Randell's circuit model, and the RCT values of the bare AC-CQDs and AD@rGO were 42 and 7 Ω, respectively. This six-times lower resistance of the composite structure shows that rGO provides a conductive network that promotes the separation of photogenerated excitons in AC-CQDs. Furthermore, under the applied voltage, which is higher than the V F , the channeling of excitons to the outer circuit is further facilitated [24]. Further, the excitons try to align themselves according to the external electric field; hence, their recombination is suppressed owing to the external electrostatic force from the biased voltage [24].

To better understand the performance of the AD-CQDs, the structure of the CQDs was studied using Raman spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. 7(e). Two main peaks were observed, originating from structural defects (D band) and graphitic planes with ideal crystallinity (G band). ID/G is the ratio of defects to graphitic planes, as shown in Fig. 7(e). The defects (higher ID/G value) in AD@rGO were higher than those in the CD@rGO structure, possibly because of the amorphous core synthesized at a lower temperature. From the literature, this type of behavior is observed when the crystalline subdomains are formed in the ring shape [55,56]. It was observed that the ring/onion-shaped carbonized carbon has a higher intensity of 002 plane and lower intensity of 111 plane in WAXD analysis. In contrast, the D band intensity of Raman analysis was higher in carbon structures with a higher carbonization degree [55,57]. This effect was related to fact that the sp2-subdomains accommodate significant content of defects so that a ring-shaped structure can be formed [58]. Interestingly, a marginal shift in the frequency of the CQDs was also observed, i.e., the G band was shifted to higher and lower frequencies in AD@rGO and CD@rGO, respectively. Such a shift to lower or higher frequencies is due to an increase in the density of electrons (n-type character) or the hole transport tendency of the material, respectively [59]. This result is consistent with the higher pyridinic-nitrogen content of CD@rGO, which increases the density of electrons near the Fermi level. In contrast, the AC-CQDs had the lower doped nitrogen in the core that is formed during low-temperature carbonization. The higher content of oxygen in the low-temperature carbonized domains renders p-type characteristics in the AC-CQDs, which reflects the characteristic shift in Raman analysis [59].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of catalysis and CQDs structure
ce:section.ce:para: Based on these results, the expected mechanism of better photoelectrocatalytic activity is depicted in Fig. 7(f). The difference in crystallinity is shown via the gradient color of AC-CQDs grown on rGO. Under the photoexcitation, the excitons are produced, which tend to align themselves according to external voltage and tilted band structure. Under these conditions, the electrons are mobile toward the rGO and CC substrate; however, the holes are available on the AC-CQDs’ surface for the degradation of organic pollutants. In order to elucidate the role of rGO and CQDs’ structure in enhancing the performance of CQDs, we tested the catalysis of each component individually. A significant improvement in catalysis was observed after attachment with rGO sheets. The CQDs without rGO offer high resistance, as confirmed from EIS analysis; hence, rGO can be considered as charge mediator and sink [60]. Due to its high tendency of charge accommodation, it serves as a charge sink, which reduces the quantum confinement effect in the CQDs. The charge tunneling effectively suppresses charge recombination.

In order to confirm the junction formation and better charge separation of the AC-CQDs, we tested the photocatalytic activity of the bare quantum dots, without rGO substrate. The better charge separation endowed a superior photocatalytic activity in the AC-CQDs (Fig. S5). For instance, under UV light irradiation of 2 h, the dye-degradation of 20% and 34% was observed in C-CQDs and AC-CQDs structure, respectively. Similarly, by keeping the 1% CQDs colloid in dye-solution under outdoor sunlight conditions, 62% and 85% degradation was observed for C-CQDs and AC-CQDs colloid respectively. This significant improvement in the dye degradation under both UV- and outdoor-light can be associated with better charge separation of AC-CQDs. It is important to note that without rGO, the activity of pure CQDs is much low therefore longer time in degradation is required. This effect is due to the reason that rGO support additionally suppresses the charge recombination.

In order to elucidate the formation mechanism, we tested the AC-CQDs after carbonization at different temperatures. For instance, the structure of AC-CQDs only after carbonization at 140 °C (AC-CQDs@140 or CPDs) (Fig. S6 (a-c)) dominantly showed an amorphous carbon skeleton. Furthermore, the amorphous structure is also obvious from the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern (Fig. S6 (d)). The fluorescence of AC-CQDs@140 showed excitation independent blue emission, attributed to molecular states from fluorophores (Fig. S7) [7]. At this stage, the AC-CQDs@140 structure can be referred to as the condensed fluorophore or CPDs. On the other hand, the CD@rGO showed a mix phase of crystalline and amorphous domains, as shown in Fig. S8. In contrast, the AC-CQDs structure showed crystalline domains well separated from amorphous domains, as shown in Fig. S9. These domains are formed in different modes, for instance, Fig. S9 (d,e) shows the formation of crystalline domains on the edges. Whereas, in some cases [Fig. S9 (c,f)], side by side alignment of domains were also formed, as shown at different resolutions in Fig. S9. Various arrangements of amorphous and crystalline subdomains were observed in AC-CQDs: side to side arrangement, core-shell structure, and minor content of QDs with only amorphous or only crystalline domains. In order to completely understand the formation mechanism, the synthesis of bare AC-CQDs (without rGO) is necessary (which is not considered in the scope of this work). From the results of this study, we can consider that the initial low temperature and final high temperature provide a structure having better separation of amorphous and crystalline subdomains.

Also, the carbonization of CQDs synthesized at 200 °C (CQDs-200) only were studied (Fig. S10). Morphology of such CQDs-200 resembled the fused- carbon rings or -carbon onions structure. Most of them existed separately in the form of aggregates on the TEM grid (Fig. S10 (a)). However, minor growth of CQDs-200 was observed on the graphene sheets, as shown in Figs. S10(b and c). The crystal fringes were forming concentric circular rings when CQDs-200 were observed at high resolution, as shown in Fig. S10(c). Dominantly crystalline morphology of these CQDs-200 is obvious from the SAED image (Fig. S10 inset). Hence, it can be deduced that with these reactants and under high-temperature conditions, the ring-like sp2-crystalline rings are formed. Therefore, the crystalline surface is formed in AC-CQDs in the form of a ring. The amorphous carbon structure is formed at low temperatures (CQDs@140) and sp2-crystalline carbon-nano-onions structure is formed at elevated temperatures (CQDs@200). Compared to CQDs@140 (Fig. S6) and CQDs@200 (Fig. S10), a biphase structure of AC-CQDs (Fig. S9) offers better photocatalytic activity. As the reaction was carried out without stirring, hence the reaction process is diffusion controlled. Over time, reduction of graphene oxide also occurs, due to which it tends to settle down, hence the growth at the bottom is passivated by settled down network of rGO. The variation in the reaction kinetics in different sections results in the formation of different structures. Also, we didn't apply the chromatographic or dialysis membrane separation techniques, hence the variation in size and structure was observed in both composite structures. From the TEM of CD@rGO (Fig. S8) and AD@rGO (Fig. S9), it can be inferred that C-CQDs have mixed phase of crystalline and amorphous domains, whereas the gradient heating induced the formation of separate amorphous domains. In some cases, these amorphous domains were in the center; whereas in some cases, side-by-side arrangements were also observed.

A facile strategy to control the crystal structure of CQDs was proposed, which can provide better photoelectrocatalytic activity. The gradient carbonization temperature, which is low at the beginning and high at the end of the process, was responsible for the formation of the proposed AD-CQD structure. The optical characterizations revealed that a longer lifetime was offered by the AD-CQDs, which could be associated with junction formation, which resulted in channeling of charge, thereby suppressing the fast recombination. The XPS and Raman analyses confirmed that the AD-CQDs had a higher content of oxygenated carbon (synthesized at a lower temperature), which can be associated with the formation of a p-type domain. Conversely, high-temperature carbonization formed nitrogen-rich carbon, which serves as an n-type domain. This junction formation is responsible for the 100% degradation of dye in 40 min, whereas 70% dye degradation was observed in 60 min for the electrode with uniform heating. Moreover, the electrophotocatalytic activity of the electrode was recorded to be 100% degradation in 25 min under an applied voltage of 0.35 V. Our strategy provides a one-step direct synthesis of CQDs on rGO, with high cyclic stability, which can pave the way for extending the applications of metal-free catalysts.

The simulation of better catalytic activity of bare AC-CQDs (without rGO), inverse temperature (high to low temperature) gradient structures, and AC-CQDs formed with other precursors remain future work. Elucidation of the formation mechanism (without using rGO) is another perspective for the further extension of this work. The role of surface states using different solvents can also be combined to see the effect of gradient solvent variations (not only temperature) to further enhance the charge channeling.

Mumtaz Ali: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, Investigation. Aima Sameen Anjum: Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation. Ayesha Bibi: Formal analysis, Writing and Analysis assistance. S. Wageh: Validation, Writing and Analysis assistance. Kyung Chul Sun: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Sung Hoon Jeong: Supervision, Project administration.
